items.creator: Greg Kohs (dir.) / DeepMind
items.summary: A documentary on DeepMind's AlphaGo defeating Go champion Lee Sedol, including the famous 'Move 37.'
key_takeaways: Captures a landmark moment of machine creativity
'Move 37' as a symbol of non-human intuition
items.why_it_matters: A vivid, emotional reference point for what AI surpassing humans actually felt like.
items.conversation_note: Everyone knows 'Move 37' — an instant shared reference for AI creativity.

items.title: Why Software Is Eating the World
items.creator: Marc Andreessen
items.summary: The essay that argued software companies would disrupt and absorb traditional industries across the economy.
key_takeaways: Software as the universal disruptor of incumbents
Framed a generation of vertical-SaaS theses
Now a default lens for tech investing
items.why_it_matters: One of the most-cited tech theses of the decade; shorthand everyone in the Valley understands.
items.conversation_note: The phrase is industry shorthand; everyone can riff on what software ate next.

items.title: It's Time to Build
items.creator: Marc Andreessen
items.summary: A pandemic-era call to arms arguing the West has lost its capacity to build housing, infrastructure, and institutions.
key_takeaways: Diagnoses an institutional failure to build
A rallying cry for ambition and agency
items.why_it_matters: It seeded the 'builder' identity and progress-studies movement now common in SV.
items.conversation_note: Cited approvingly and mockingly — either way it sparks a real debate about why we can't build.

items.title: The Almanack of Naval Ravikant
items.creator: Eric Jorgenson
items.summary: A curated collection of Naval Ravikant's thinking on wealth creation, leverage, and happiness.
key_takeaways: 'Productize yourself' via specific knowledge and leverage
Seek wealth, not money or status
Happiness as a skill and choice
items.why_it_matters: Naval's aphorisms are near-universal cultural currency among founders and operators.
items.conversation_note: Quoting Naval is a SV social lubricant; everyone has a favorite line.

items.title: High Output Management
items.creator: Andy Grove
items.summary: Intel CEO Andy Grove's classic on managing for output, leverage, and high-performance teams.
key_takeaways: Managerial output = output of your org + orgs you influence
Meetings, 1:1s, and decisions as leverage tools
items.why_it_matters: The management bible repeatedly endorsed by top founders and investors.
items.conversation_note: The default answer to 'what management book actually matters' — a credibility signal.

items.title: Only the Paranoid Survive
items.creator: Andy Grove
items.summary: Andy Grove on navigating 'strategic inflection points' — moments when the fundamentals of a business shift.
key_takeaways: Paranoia as disciplined attention to change
Intel's memory-to-microprocessor pivot as case study
items.why_it_matters: 'Strategic inflection point' is durable SV vocabulary, freshly relevant in the AI shift.
items.conversation_note: Frame AI as the current 'strategic inflection point' and you've started a strategy debate.

items.title: 1,000 True Fans
items.creator: Kevin Kelly
items.summary: The essay arguing a creator needs only ~1,000 true fans paying meaningfully to make a living.
key_takeaways: Direct relationships beat mass reach for creators
Niche + depth can sustain a business
items.why_it_matters: A foundational idea behind the creator economy and many bottoms-up businesses.
items.conversation_note: A clean lens for debating the creator economy and niche business models.

items.title: Super Founders
items.creator: Ali Tamaseb
items.summary: A data-driven study comparing billion-dollar startup founders against a control group, debunking common myths.
key_takeaways: Many 'founder myths' don't survive the data
Age, pedigree, and idea novelty matter less than assumed
items.why_it_matters: Gives founders and investors actual data to argue against lazy pattern-matching.
items.conversation_note: Drop a counterintuitive stat and watch investors defend their pattern-matching.

items.title: Hooked: How to Build Habit-Forming Products
items.creator: Nir Eyal
items.summary: A model (trigger, action, variable reward, investment) for building products that form habits.
key_takeaways: The Hook Model of habit formation
Raises the ethics of persuasive design
items.why_it_matters: Defines the engagement-design playbook — and the ethical backlash against it.
items.conversation_note: Sets up the 'is engagement design manipulation?' debate.

items.title: Obviously Awesome
items.creator: April Dunford
items.summary: A practical method for positioning products so customers instantly understand their value.
key_takeaways: Pick the market frame you want to win
Reposition rather than rebuild when stuck
items.why_it_matters: The go-to reference when teams argue about positioning and category.
items.conversation_note: A sharp lens for critiquing any startup's confusing positioning.

items.title: The Tyranny of the Marginal User
items.creator: Ivan Vendrov
items.summary: An essay arguing that optimizing for the least-engaged marginal user degrades products for everyone.
key_takeaways: Growth metrics pull products toward the lowest common denominator
Explains why beloved apps get worse
Names a force every PM has felt
items.why_it_matters: A crisp explanation for 'enshittification' that product people love to debate.
items.conversation_note: Explains why everyone's favorite app got worse — universally relatable.

items.title: Amazon Shareholder Letters
items.creator: Jeff Bezos
items.summary: Bezos's annual letters (1997–2020) laying out long-term thinking, customer obsession, and Day 1 culture.
items.why_it_matters: The most-cited primary source on operating philosophy at scale.
items.conversation_note: Citing 'Day 1' or a Bezos letter signals you study operating excellence.

items.title: No Silver Bullet — Essence and Accident in Software Engineering
items.creator: Fred Brooks
items.summary: Brooks argues no single technique will yield an order-of-magnitude productivity gain because software's hard parts are essential, not accidental.
key_takeaways: A recurring lens for evaluating hype
items.why_it_matters: The default rebuttal to every 'this tool will 10x developers' claim — newly relevant for AI coding.
items.conversation_note: The perfect skeptic's reply to 'AI will 10x engineering.'

items.title: Reflections on Trusting Trust
items.creator: Ken Thompson
items.summary: Ken Thompson's Turing Award lecture showing you can't fully trust code you didn't create yourself, via a self-replicating compiler backdoor.
key_takeaways: A compiler can hide an undetectable backdoor
items.why_it_matters: The canonical reference for software trust and supply-chain security debates.
items.conversation_note: A mind-bending classic that makes any security conversation deeper.

items.title: The Rise of Worse Is Better
items.creator: Richard P. Gabriel
items.summary: The essay contrasting 'the right thing' with 'worse is better,' arguing simpler, less-perfect designs often win.
key_takeaways: Simplicity of implementation can beat completeness
A lasting frame for design tradeoffs
items.why_it_matters: A perennial lens for debating why uglier, simpler tech keeps winning.
items.conversation_note: Explains why the 'worse' product keeps beating the elegant one.

items.title: Site Reliability Engineering (The SRE Book)
items.creator: Google (Beyer, Jones, Petoff, Murphy, eds.)
items.summary: Google's account of running production systems at scale: SLOs, error budgets, and toil reduction.
key_takeaways: Error budgets balance velocity and reliability
items.why_it_matters: It defined modern reliability vocabulary used across infra teams.
items.conversation_note: 'Error budgets' is the phrase that signals you've run real production systems.

items.title: A Philosophy of Software Design
items.creator: John Ousterhout
items.summary: A Stanford professor's take on managing complexity through deep modules and good abstractions.
key_takeaways: Complexity is the enemy; manage it deliberately
Prefer deep modules with simple interfaces
items.why_it_matters: A modern, contrarian-to-Clean-Code reference that sparks engineering debate.
items.conversation_note: Pit it against 'Clean Code' and engineers will happily argue for an hour.

items.title: The Twelve-Factor App
items.creator: Adam Wiggins / Heroku
items.summary: A methodology of twelve principles for building portable, scalable web apps in the cloud era.
key_takeaways: Config in the environment, not code
items.why_it_matters: Shaped how a generation built cloud apps; still a baseline reference.
items.conversation_note: A quick way to check if someone has actually shipped cloud software.

items.title: Bad Blood: Secrets and Lies in a Silicon Valley Startup
items.creator: John Carreyrou
items.summary: The definitive account of Theranos's rise and fraud, by the journalist who broke the story.
key_takeaways: 'Fake it till you make it' taken to fraud
A cautionary tale on founder mythology
items.why_it_matters: The ultimate cautionary tale about hype, governance, and the dark side of founder worship.
items.conversation_note: Universally read; a safe, juicy reference for the limits of hype.

items.title: The Everything Store: Jeff Bezos and the Age of Amazon
items.creator: Brad Stone
items.summary: A deeply reported history of Amazon's rise and Bezos's relentless operating culture.
items.why_it_matters: The reference history for Amazon's strategy and culture, endlessly cited in operating debates.
items.conversation_note: Pairs perfectly with the Bezos letters for an Amazon-strategy conversation.

items.title: Chaos Monkeys
items.creator: Antonio García Martínez
items.summary: A caustic, insider memoir of startup life, YC, and ad-tech inside Facebook.
key_takeaways: Unsparing look at startup and Big-Tech incentives
Ad-tech and monetization from the inside
items.why_it_matters: A polarizing insider account that people love to argue about for its candor and controversy.
items.conversation_note: Polarizing — mention it and people immediately take sides.

items.title: Valley of Genius
items.creator: Adam Fisher
items.summary: An oral history of Silicon Valley told through the voices of the people who built it.
key_takeaways: First-person, contradictory accounts of key moments
Atari to Apple to Google to social
Myth vs. memory in tech history
items.why_it_matters: A lively, quotable source of Valley lore and origin stories.
items.conversation_note: A goldmine of origin-story anecdotes to drop at dinner.

items.title: The Dream Machine
items.creator: M. Mitchell Waldrop
items.summary: The story of J.C.R. Licklider and the visionaries who shaped personal and networked computing.
key_takeaways: Government research seeded the PC and internet
items.why_it_matters: A beloved deep history that signals real literacy in where computing came from.
items.conversation_note: Citing Licklider and 'The Dream Machine' is a deep-cut credibility move.

items.title: The Social Network
items.creator: David Fincher (dir.) / Aaron Sorkin
items.summary: The dramatized origin story of Facebook, founder conflict, and the cost of ambition.
key_takeaways: Myth vs. reality of origin stories
items.why_it_matters: The most culturally pervasive depiction of startup founding — shared reference for everyone.
items.conversation_note: Everyone's seen it — an effortless way to open a founder-culture conversation.

items.title: Pirates of Silicon Valley
items.creator: Martyn Burke (dir.)
items.summary: A docudrama on the Apple–Microsoft rivalry and the personalities of Jobs and Gates.
key_takeaways: Borrowing and competition in early PCs
items.why_it_matters: A nostalgic touchstone for the founding rivalry that shaped personal computing.
items.conversation_note: A fun deep cut for the Jobs-vs-Gates origin debate.

items.title: 7 Powers: The Foundations of Business Strategy
items.creator: Hamilton Helmer
items.summary: A framework of seven durable sources of competitive advantage (power) and when each applies.
key_takeaways: Timing determines which power is available
items.why_it_matters: The most precise shared vocabulary for moats, beloved by investors and strategists.
items.conversation_note: Naming a startup's specific 'power' is a sharp, status-y way to analyze moats.

items.title: Aggregation Theory
items.creator: Ben Thompson (Stratechery)
items.summary: The theory that internet-era winners aggregate demand by owning the user relationship and commoditizing supply.
items.why_it_matters: The dominant strategic framework for platform competition in the modern Valley.
items.conversation_note: 'Aggregator vs. platform' is core Valley vocabulary — wield it and you sound fluent.

items.title: Amazon's Antitrust Paradox
items.creator: Lina Khan
items.summary: The Yale Law Journal note arguing consumer-welfare antitrust fails to capture Amazon's structural power.
key_takeaways: Argues for structural views of platform power
Reshaped US antitrust thinking and policy
items.why_it_matters: It moved tech antitrust from academic to live policy — its author became FTC chair.
items.conversation_note: The reference point for any Big-Tech regulation debate.

items.title: Competing Against Luck (Jobs to Be Done)
items.creator: Clayton Christensen et al.
items.summary: Christensen's theory that customers 'hire' products to do a job, reframing how to find demand.
items.why_it_matters: JTBD is everyday product vocabulary; the milkshake story is a shared reference.
items.conversation_note: Tell the milkshake story and you've started a product-thinking conversation.

items.title: The Age of Surveillance Capitalism
items.creator: Shoshana Zuboff
items.summary: An argument that tech giants commodify behavioral data into 'behavioral futures' markets.
key_takeaways: Behavioral surplus as the new raw material
Prediction products sold in futures markets
A systemic critique of ad-driven tech
items.why_it_matters: The most-cited intellectual critique of the ad-driven internet's business model.
items.conversation_note: The heavyweight reference for critiquing the attention economy.

items.title: Weapons of Math Destruction
items.creator: Cathy O'Neil
items.summary: How opaque algorithms in credit, policing, and hiring can scale bias and inequality.
items.why_it_matters: A widely-read primer for the fairness and accountability side of the AI debate.
items.conversation_note: An accessible entry to the algorithmic-fairness conversation.

items.title: The Social Dilemma
items.creator: Jeff Orlowski (dir.)
items.summary: A documentary in which former insiders critique social media's attention and manipulation mechanics.
items.why_it_matters: The mainstream on-ramp to the social-media-harms debate that most people have seen.
items.conversation_note: Widely watched — easy to reference, easy to debate (insiders vs. overstated).

items.title: The Californian Ideology
items.creator: Richard Barbrook & Andy Cameron
items.summary: A prescient critique of Silicon Valley's fusion of countercultural libertarianism and free-market tech optimism.
key_takeaways: A lens on tech's political identity
items.why_it_matters: It named the Valley's worldview decades early; still cited to explain tech politics.
items.conversation_note: A sophisticated frame for discussing tech's politics without getting partisan.

items.title: The Attention Merchants
items.creator: Tim Wu
items.summary: A history of how industries from newspapers to social media have harvested and resold human attention.
items.why_it_matters: Gives historical depth to the attention-economy conversation that dominates tech.
items.conversation_note: Adds historical heft to any attention-economy debate.

items.title: Snow Crash
items.creator: Neal Stephenson
items.summary: The cyberpunk novel that coined the 'Metaverse' and shaped how technologists imagine virtual worlds.
key_takeaways: Influenced VR, crypto, and gaming visions
Sci-fi as a roadmap technologists actually follow
items.why_it_matters: Required cultural reading behind metaverse and VR ambitions; a shared imaginative reference.
items.conversation_note: Explains where 'metaverse' came from — a fun bridge from fiction to product.

items.title: Meditations on Moloch
items.creator: Scott Alexander
items.summary: An essay using 'Moloch' as a metaphor for coordination failures where everyone races to the bottom.
key_takeaways: Multipolar traps and races to the bottom
Why good actors produce bad equilibria
A vocabulary for systemic incentive failure
items.why_it_matters: 'Moloch' is shorthand in SV intellectual circles, frequently invoked in AI-race debates.
items.conversation_note: Saying 'that's a Moloch problem' is a high-status SV intellectual move.

items.title: The Beginning of Infinity
items.creator: David Deutsch
items.summary: A physicist's argument that good explanations drive unbounded knowledge and progress.
key_takeaways: Good explanations as the engine of progress
items.why_it_matters: A favorite of the progress-and-optimism crowd (Naval, founders); a worldview reference.
items.conversation_note: Name-checking Deutsch signals you're in the progress-studies / optimist camp.

items.title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
items.creator: Daniel Kahneman
items.summary: The Nobel laureate's synthesis of System 1 / System 2 thinking and cognitive biases.
key_takeaways: Fast intuitive vs. slow deliberate thinking
items.why_it_matters: The shared vocabulary (System 1/2, biases) underlying most product and decision conversations.
items.conversation_note: System 1/2 is near-universal vocabulary — safe, smart, always relevant.

items.title: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System
items.creator: Satoshi Nakamoto
items.summary: The nine-page whitepaper that introduced Bitcoin and a working solution to double-spending without a trusted third party.
key_takeaways: Proof-of-work and a public ledger solve double-spending
The founding document of an entire industry
items.why_it_matters: The origin text of crypto; you can't seriously discuss the space without it.
items.conversation_note: The primary source — citing it (vs. price chatter) signals you actually understand crypto.

items.title: Ethereum Whitepaper
items.creator: Vitalik Buterin
items.summary: Vitalik Buterin's proposal for a programmable blockchain with a Turing-complete scripting language.
key_takeaways: Generalizes blockchains from money to arbitrary programs
Smart contracts and dapps as primitives
Foundation for DeFi, NFTs, and DAOs
items.why_it_matters: It reframed blockchains as world computers, defining the second era of crypto.
items.conversation_note: Sets up the 'is web3 a platform or a casino' debate from first principles.

items.title: The Scalability Trilemma
items.creator: Vitalik Buterin
items.summary: The framing that blockchains struggle to be simultaneously scalable, secure, and decentralized — you trade off among the three.
key_takeaways: Scalability, security, decentralization as a trilemma
Explains L2s, rollups, and sharding choices
The core technical tension of crypto design
items.why_it_matters: It is the precise vocabulary for the scaling-vs-decentralization argument that defines crypto engineering.
items.conversation_note: Name the trilemma and you've elevated a crypto chat from price to architecture.

items.title: Going Infinite: The Rise and Fall of a New Tycoon
items.creator: Michael Lewis
items.summary: Michael Lewis's close-up account of Sam Bankman-Fried and the collapse of FTX.
key_takeaways: An inside view of FTX's rise and implosion
Effective altruism, risk, and hubris collide
A polarizing portrait that splits readers
items.why_it_matters: FTX is the defining crypto scandal; Lewis's controversial take is itself a debate.
items.conversation_note: Everyone has a hot take on SBF and whether Lewis went too easy on him.

items.title: Number Go Up
items.creator: Zeke Faux
items.summary: An investigative romp through the crypto bubble, Tether, and the industry's seedier corners.
key_takeaways: Skeptical, on-the-ground reporting on crypto mania
Hard questions about Tether and stablecoins
items.why_it_matters: The best-known skeptical narrative — essential for arguing the bear case credibly.
items.conversation_note: Pair it with a believer's book and you have a built-in crypto debate.

items.title: Read Write Own
items.creator: Chris Dixon
items.summary: a16z's Chris Dixon makes the case for blockchains as networks that let users own a piece of the internet.
key_takeaways: 'Read, write, own' as the third internet era
Tokens align networks with their users
The strongest articulation of the pro-web3 thesis
items.why_it_matters: The clearest statement of the optimistic web3 case from its biggest backer.
items.conversation_note: The believer's case — debate it against Molly White's ledger of disasters.

items.title: Web3 Is Going Just Great
items.creator: Molly White
items.summary: A continuously updated catalog of crypto hacks, scams, and collapses, with tallied losses.
key_takeaways: A running tally of crypto's failures
The most-cited critical resource in the space
items.why_it_matters: The definitive skeptic's reference — a factual counterweight to web3 hype.
items.conversation_note: The receipts for anyone arguing crypto is mostly grift.

items.title: Chip War: The Fight for the World's Most Critical Technology
items.creator: Chris Miller
items.summary: A history of the semiconductor industry and the US–China struggle for control of advanced chips.
key_takeaways: Chips as the oil of the 21st century
TSMC, ASML, and choke points in the supply chain
items.why_it_matters: The definitive primer on the compute supply chain everyone now debates.
items.conversation_note: The shared reference for the chips-and-geopolitics conversation — guaranteed to land.

items.title: Acquired: TSMC
items.creator: Acquired (Ben Gilbert & David Rosenthal)
items.summary: A long-form history of how Morris Chang built TSMC into the world's most important company.
key_takeaways: The pure-play foundry model that reshaped chips
Why the world depends on one Taiwanese firm
Strategy lessons in patient, capital-intensive scale
items.why_it_matters: The most accessible deep-dive on the single most strategically important company in tech.
items.conversation_note: Knowing the Morris Chang / TSMC story is table stakes for chip conversations.

items.title: Acquired: NVIDIA
items.creator: Acquired (Ben Gilbert & David Rosenthal)
items.summary: A multi-part history of NVIDIA's path from gaming GPUs to the backbone of the AI boom.
key_takeaways: CUDA and the bet on general-purpose GPU compute
How gaming hardware became AI infrastructure
items.why_it_matters: NVIDIA is the company at the center of the AI era; this is the canonical narrative.
items.conversation_note: Explains why NVIDIA, not the model labs, captured so much AI value.

items.title: Cramming More Components onto Integrated Circuits
items.creator: Gordon Moore
items.summary: The paper that introduced what became Moore's Law — the doubling of transistor density over time.
key_takeaways: The empirical 'law' that drove five decades of progress
Exponential improvement as an industry organizing principle
The debate over whether it is ending
items.why_it_matters: The single most influential prediction in computing; its slowdown reframes everything.
items.conversation_note: 'Is Moore's Law dead?' is an evergreen, opinion-splitting question.

items.title: The Idea Factory: Bell Labs and the Great Age of American Innovation
items.creator: Jon Gertner
items.summary: The story of Bell Labs and how it produced the transistor, the laser, information theory, and more.
key_takeaways: How a single lab invented the modern world
A model (and warning) for industrial R&D
items.why_it_matters: The reference point for debates about where real innovation comes from.
items.conversation_note: Fuels the 'why don't we build Bell Labs anymore' debate.

items.title: How the World Really Works
items.creator: Vaclav Smil
items.summary: Vaclav Smil's data-grounded account of energy, food, materials, and the physical realities behind modern life.
key_takeaways: Fossil fuels underpin food, steel, cement, plastics
Energy transitions are slow and material-intensive
A sober counter to techno-optimist timelines
items.why_it_matters: Smil is the energy realist that founders (and Bill Gates) cite to check climate hype.
items.conversation_note: The go-to for puncturing both climate doom and naive techno-optimism.

items.title: Where Is My Flying Car?
items.creator: J. Storrs Hall
items.summary: An argument that energy and atom-world progress stalled, with nanotech and nuclear as the unrealized future.
key_takeaways: Cheap, abundant energy as the missing input
A cult favorite of the progress movement
items.why_it_matters: A beloved SV deep-tech text that frames the 'why did atoms stop progressing' debate.
items.audience_fit: Founders, deep-tech builders, the progress crowd
items.conversation_note: Catnip for the progress-studies crowd; instantly starts an 'are we stagnating' debate.

items.title: How to Avoid a Climate Disaster
items.creator: Bill Gates
items.summary: Bill Gates's framework for getting to net-zero, organized around 'green premiums' and hard-to-abate sectors.
key_takeaways: Innovation, not just deployment, is needed
items.why_it_matters: A widely-read, investor-friendly map of the climate-tech opportunity space.
items.conversation_note: 'Green premium' gives a shared lens for evaluating any climate startup.

items.title: Speed & Scale
items.creator: John Doerr
items.summary: Venture investor John Doerr applies OKRs to a global plan for reaching net-zero emissions.
key_takeaways: An OKR-driven action plan for climate
VC operating discipline applied to climate
items.why_it_matters: Connects the climate agenda to the execution language SV already speaks.
items.conversation_note: Bridges climate to OKRs — comfortable territory for operators.

items.title: The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money & Power
items.creator: Daniel Yergin
items.summary: A Pulitzer-winning history of the oil industry and how energy shaped the modern world and geopolitics.
key_takeaways: Energy as the hidden driver of geopolitics
How the oil industry built the 20th century
items.why_it_matters: The definitive backstory for anyone reasoning about energy, power, and transition.
items.conversation_note: Grounds energy-transition talk in how power and oil actually worked.

items.title: Stripe Climate & Advance Market Commitments (Frontier)
items.creator: Stripe / Frontier
items.summary: Stripe's effort to fund early carbon-removal via advance market commitments, later expanded as Frontier.
key_takeaways: Advance market commitments to pull tech into existence
Software money funding hard climate tech
A new model for funding deep tech
items.why_it_matters: A concrete, much-discussed example of tech capital trying to bootstrap a hard market.
items.conversation_note: A tidy example of using market design (not subsidies) to fund deep tech.

items.title: AI Superpowers: China, Silicon Valley, and the New World Order
items.creator: Kai-Fu Lee
items.summary: Kai-Fu Lee's comparison of the Chinese and American AI ecosystems and what each does better.
key_takeaways: Different innovation cultures, US vs China
items.why_it_matters: The reference book for the US–China AI competition framing that recurs constantly.
items.conversation_note: The starting point for any serious US-vs-China tech conversation.

items.title: The Billionaire Raj
items.creator: James Crabtree
items.summary: A portrait of India's new tycoon economy, inequality, and the conditions shaping its tech and business boom.
key_takeaways: The dynamics behind India's startup surge
Context for the world's fastest-growing internet market
items.why_it_matters: Essential background for the India-as-next-big-market thesis investors love to debate.
items.conversation_note: Adds nuance to the 'India is the next China' investor pitch.

items.title: Blitzscaling
items.creator: Reid Hoffman & Chris Yeh
items.summary: A playbook for prioritizing speed over efficiency to win winner-take-most markets, with heavy US–China framing.
key_takeaways: Prioritize speed over efficiency to capture markets
Explicit US vs China scaling comparison
items.why_it_matters: A widely-debated (and criticized) thesis on how global tech races are won.
items.conversation_note: Loved and loathed — a reliable way to start a scaling-strategy argument.

items.title: State of European Tech
items.creator: Atomico
items.summary: Atomico's annual data report on the European tech and venture ecosystem.
key_takeaways: Hard data on European funding and outcomes
Tracks the gap with the US ecosystem
items.why_it_matters: The shared factual baseline for arguing whether Europe can build globally significant tech.
items.conversation_note: Brings receipts to the perennial 'why can't Europe build a trillion-dollar company' debate.

items.title: Bullshit Jobs
items.creator: David Graeber
items.summary: Anthropologist David Graeber argues a large share of modern jobs are pointless and explains why they persist.
key_takeaways: A provocation about work and value
items.why_it_matters: A polarizing, widely-cited lens on automation, meaning, and the value of work.
items.conversation_note: Everyone has a 'bullshit job' story — and an opinion on whether theirs counts.

items.title: Remote: Office Not Required
items.creator: Jason Fried & David Heinemeier Hansson
items.summary: The 37signals founders' early, influential case for remote work and async collaboration.
key_takeaways: Remote work as default, not exception
Trust and output over hours and presence
items.why_it_matters: The pre-pandemic playbook that framed the remote-vs-office fight now central to tech.
items.conversation_note: The opening move in any remote-vs-return-to-office debate.

items.title: A World Without Email
items.creator: Cal Newport
items.summary: Cal Newport argues the 'hyperactive hive mind' of constant messaging wrecks knowledge-work productivity.
key_takeaways: Constant messaging is a productivity tax
Attention as the scarce resource of knowledge work
items.why_it_matters: A practical, widely-discussed critique of how modern teams actually (fail to) work.
items.conversation_note: Resonates with anyone drowning in Slack — easy, relatable debate.

items.title: Rise of the Robots
items.creator: Martin Ford
items.summary: An argument that automation and AI will displace large swaths of jobs across skill levels.
key_takeaways: Automation may hit white-collar work too
An early case for ideas like UBI
items.why_it_matters: A foundational text for the automation-and-jobs debate now supercharged by AI.
items.conversation_note: The on-ramp to 'will AI take the jobs' — pairs well with today's headlines.

items.title: 996.ICU
items.creator: Chinese tech workers (GitHub)
items.summary: A viral GitHub project protesting China's '996' (9am–9pm, 6 days) overwork culture in tech.
key_takeaways: '996' as shorthand for extreme hustle culture
Labor pushback inside a censored ecosystem
items.why_it_matters: A vivid global counterpoint to hustle culture and the limits of the grind.
items.conversation_note: A sharp prompt for the hustle-culture-vs-burnout debate, with a global angle.

items.title: The GitLab Handbook (All-Remote)
items.creator: GitLab
items.summary: GitLab's radically transparent, public handbook documenting how a large all-remote company actually runs.
key_takeaways: Concrete async and remote operating practices
items.why_it_matters: The most-cited real-world reference for how to run an all-remote company at scale.
items.conversation_note: The 'show me a remote company that works' answer — concrete, not theoretical.

items.title: Unit X: How the Pentagon and Silicon Valley Are Transforming the Future of War
items.creator: Raj M. Shah & Christopher Kirchhoff
items.summary: An inside account of the Defense Innovation Unit's effort to bring commercial tech speed into the Pentagon.
key_takeaways: How commercial tech is entering defense
items.why_it_matters: The clearest insider narrative on the defense-tech wave drawing Valley founders and capital.
items.conversation_note: The reference for why defense tech suddenly became cool in the Valley.

items.title: The Kill Chain: Defending America in the Future of High-Tech Warfare
items.creator: Christian Brose
items.summary: A former Senate staffer argues US defense must shift from expensive platforms to networked, software-defined systems.
items.why_it_matters: Shapes the thesis behind Anduril-style defense startups and the software-eats-defense argument.
items.conversation_note: The intellectual backbone of the new defense-tech startups.

items.title: Recoding America
items.creator: Jennifer Pahlka
items.summary: The Code for America founder on why government technology fails and how to actually fix delivery.
key_takeaways: Policy and implementation are fatally separated
Delivery as a core governing capability
items.why_it_matters: The defining modern text on govtech and state capacity, cited across the political spectrum.
items.conversation_note: The 'why is government software so bad' conversation, answered seriously.

items.title: The Pentagon's Brain
items.creator: Annie Jacobsen
items.summary: A history of DARPA and its role in everything from the internet to autonomous weapons.
key_takeaways: DARPA's outsized role in modern tech
The blurry line between defense and civilian tech
items.why_it_matters: Context for DARPA's role in tech history and today's renewed state–startup ties.
items.conversation_note: Reminds everyone how much 'commercial' tech started as defense research.

items.title: Rebuild the Arsenal of Democracy (Anduril)
items.creator: Palmer Luckey / Anduril
items.summary: Anduril's public argument for software-defined, commercially-built weapons and a reformed defense industrial base.
items.why_it_matters: A lightning-rod statement of the new, openly patriotic defense-tech posture in the Valley.
items.conversation_note: Splits the room on whether founders should build weapons — a live SV fault line.

items.title: The 2026 AI Index Report
items.creator: Stanford HAI
items.summary: Stanford HAI's 2026 edition tracking AI capability, adoption, investment, safety, and the widening gap between what AI can do and how prepared institutions are to govern it.
key_takeaways: SWE-bench Verified coding jumped from ~60% to near 100% in a year
Generative AI hit 53% population adoption — faster than PC or internet
US private AI investment ($285.9B in 2025) dwarfs China; documented AI incidents rose to 362
items.why_it_matters: The current shared factual baseline for any serious AI strategy or policy conversation.
items.audience_fit: Founders, investors, researchers, operators, policy people
items.conversation_note: The freshest data everyone cites — citing 2026 figures (not 2023 vibes) marks you as current.

items.title: The Adolescence of Technology
items.creator: Dario Amodei
items.summary: Anthropic's CEO argues AI is in a volatile 'adolescent' phase, analyzing risks of misuse, misalignment, and concentration of power — and how to defend against them.
key_takeaways: Frames the present as a risky technological adolescence
Names misuse, misalignment, and power-concentration as core risks
Predicts human-level AI within roughly two years
items.why_it_matters: A January 2026 statement from a frontier-lab CEO that set the year's AI-risk agenda.
items.conversation_note: The 2026 counterweight to AI optimism — pairs directly against Altman's 'Gentle Singularity.'

items.title: The Gentle Singularity
items.creator: Sam Altman
items.summary: OpenAI's CEO argues the singularity will feel gradual and manageable, with agents doing cognitive work in 2025, novel insights in 2026, and real-world robots by 2027.
key_takeaways: Exponential progress feels smooth from the inside
A year-by-year capability timeline (agents → insights → robots)
A deliberately calming counter to AI-anxiety narratives
items.why_it_matters: The optimist pole of the 2025–26 AI debate, widely read and widely criticized.
items.conversation_note: The optimist case — set it against Amodei or Yudkowsky for an instant debate.

items.title: AI 2027
items.creator: AI Futures Project (Kokotajlo, Lifland, Larsen, Dean)
items.summary: A detailed narrative scenario of AI progress from 2025 to 2027, driven by US–China race dynamics, with branching good and catastrophic endings.
key_takeaways: Race dynamics as the key accelerant and danger
Read by over a million people, including policymakers
items.why_it_matters: The most-discussed AI forecast of 2025; a reference point researchers say 'feels less wild' than the public thinks.
items.conversation_note: Everyone in AI has a take on whether the 2027 timeline is prophetic or hype.

items.title: If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies
items.creator: Eliezer Yudkowsky & Nate Soares
items.summary: A New York Times bestseller arguing that building artificial superintelligence with current methods would almost certainly destroy humanity, and calling for a global halt.
key_takeaways: Modern AI is grown, not coded — and opaque
Argues alignment is unsolved and default outcomes are lethal
Calls for coordinated global halt of large-scale AI
items.why_it_matters: The 2025 mainstream-bestseller version of the doomer case — and a lightning rod for rebuttal.
items.conversation_note: Provocative title alone starts arguments; the bestseller everyone loves or loathes.

items.title: Empire of AI
items.creator: Karen Hao
items.summary: A bestselling investigation of how OpenAI went from idealistic nonprofit to one of tech's most powerful companies, including the chips, energy, and labor behind modern AI.
key_takeaways: The hidden resource costs: chips, energy, Global South labor
A critical lens on AI's concentration of power
items.why_it_matters: The defining critical narrative of the AI boom; an NBCC finalist and instant bestseller.
items.conversation_note: The book the AI industry argues about — supplies the critical case in any OpenAI conversation.

items.title: AI Engineering: Building Applications with Foundation Models
items.creator: Chip Huyen
items.summary: A practical, framework-first guide to building applications on foundation models — prompting, RAG, fine-tuning, agents, evaluation, and deployment.
key_takeaways: Durable mental models over ephemeral tool tutorials
Strong on evaluation and dataset engineering
items.why_it_matters: The most-read book on O'Reilly and the current standard reference for the AI-engineering role.
items.audience_fit: AI engineers, technical founders, applied ML teams
items.conversation_note: The builder's reference — citing it signals you actually ship AI products.

items.title: The State of AI Report 2025
items.creator: Nathan Benaich / Air Street Capital
items.summary: The eighth annual open-access report synthesizing AI research, industry, politics, and safety, plus a survey of 1,200 practitioners.
key_takeaways: 44% of US businesses now pay for AI (up from 5% in 2023)
Average AI contract value hit $530k, projected past $1M in 2026
Tracks Stargate ($500B, 10GW US mega-cluster) and compute buildout
items.why_it_matters: The investor-grade annual synthesis that frames the year's AI market debates.
items.conversation_note: The investor-flavored counterpart to the AI Index — great for market-trend talk.

items.title: Nexus: A Brief History of Information Networks
items.creator: Yuval Noah Harari
items.summary: A sweeping history of information networks from myth and writing to AI, arguing that how societies handle information determines their fate.
key_takeaways: Information networks as the engine of history
AI as a new kind of non-human agent in those networks
Warnings about truth, control, and alignment at civilizational scale
items.why_it_matters: A mainstream big-think frame that puts AI in deep historical context — heavily referenced.
items.conversation_note: A crowd-pleasing way to zoom out from 'AI tools' to 'AI and the fate of societies.'

items.title: NVIDIA GTC 2026 Keynote: The Age of Physical AI
items.creator: Jensen Huang / NVIDIA
items.summary: Jensen Huang's March 2026 keynote reframing AI as 'AI factories,' agents, and physical AI — robots and autonomous vehicles at commercial scale.
key_takeaways: 'AI factories' churning out tokens as the new computing model
Physical AI / robotics pitched as a $1T+ market
Vera Rubin platform and an expanded RoboTaxi partner roster
items.why_it_matters: The clearest signal of where compute and robotics are heading, from the company at the center of it all.
items.conversation_note: 'Physical AI' is the 2026 buzzphrase — this keynote is where the robotics debate starts.

items.title: The GENIUS Act
items.creator: U.S. Congress
items.summary: The first major US federal crypto law, creating a framework for dollar-backed stablecoins with 1:1 reserves, audits, and disclosures.
key_takeaways: Clarifies compliant stablecoins are neither securities nor commodities
items.why_it_matters: Moved US crypto from regulatory limbo to law; global crypto briefly topped $4T after enactment.
items.conversation_note: The pivot point for 'is crypto finally legitimate' debates with institutions.

items.title: The AI Doc: Or How I Became an Apocaloptimist
items.creator: Daniel Roher & Charlie Tyrell (dir.)
items.summary: A Sundance 2026 documentary in which filmmaker Daniel Roher, expecting a child, interviews leading AI figures about the technology's threats and promises.
key_takeaways: A personal, accessible tour of the AI debate
Frames AI stakes through impending fatherhood
items.why_it_matters: A theatrical-release 2026 doc that brings the insider AI debate to a mainstream audience.
items.conversation_note: A shared 2026 cultural reference for the optimist-vs-doomer split.

items.title: Ghost in the Machine
items.creator: Valerie Veatch (dir.)
items.summary: A Sundance 2026 essay-documentary tracing AI's roots through the histories of computing, statistics, and eugenics, and the power dynamics behind the AI boom.
key_takeaways: Links AI's lineage to control and scientific racism
Connects computing, semiconductors, and statistics histories
A critical, contrarian counter-history of AI
items.why_it_matters: A provocative 2026 critical history that fuels the 'whose values are baked into AI' debate.
items.conversation_note: A sharp counterpoint that complicates triumphalist AI origin stories.
